title: Add Abstract Factory design pattern with UML, code, and examples
description: CRITICAL GAP: Abstract Factory is GoF pattern #3, ESSENTIAL consensus, present in every competitor (refactoring.guru, VisuAlgo, dofactory). Architex has Factory Method but not Abstract Factory. Add: UML classes (AbstractFactory, ConcreteFactory1, ConcreteFactory2, AbstractProductA/B, ConcreteProducts), relationships (inheritance + creation), TS/Python code, 3 real-world examples, whenToUse/whenNotToUse. Source: https://singhajit.com/gang-of-four-design-patterns/
acceptanceCriteria: Abstract Factory appears in Creational category in sidebar
UML diagram with AbstractFactory, 2 ConcreteFactories, 2 AbstractProducts, 4 ConcreteProducts
TypeScript and Python code samples both functional
3 real-world examples (UI toolkit families, database drivers, cross-platform components)
whenToUse and whenNotToUse arrays populated
Comparison note: 'vs Factory Method' explaining when to use which

title: Add Composite design pattern with UML, code, and examples
description: CRITICAL GAP: Composite is GoF structural pattern, ESSENTIAL consensus. File System problem (in our SEO data) explicitly requires Composite pattern. Referenced in lld-problems-data.ts line 470 (keyPatterns: ['Composite']). Add: UML with Component interface, Leaf, Composite classes. Source: https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/system-design/gang-of-four-gof-design-patterns/
acceptanceCriteria: Composite appears in Structural category in sidebar
UML diagram with Component (interface), Leaf, Composite classes
Tree structure relationship (Composite contains Component[])
TypeScript and Python code with file system example
3 real-world examples (file systems, UI component trees, org charts)
Connection note to File System LLD problem

title: Add Chain of Responsibility pattern with UML, code, and examples
description: CRITICAL GAP: Chain of Responsibility is GoF behavioral, ESSENTIAL consensus. ATM problem (in our data) lists it as keyPattern. Express middleware, logging frameworks, event handling all use it. Add: UML with Handler interface, ConcreteHandlerA/B/C, Client. Source: https://springframework.guru/gang-of-four-design-patterns/
acceptanceCriteria: Chain of Responsibility appears in Behavioral category in sidebar
UML diagram with Handler (abstract), 3 ConcreteHandlers, Client
Each handler has 'successor' reference forming the chain
TypeScript and Python code with middleware/logging example
3 real-world examples (Express middleware, DOM event bubbling, approval workflows)
Connection note to ATM and Logging Framework LLD problems

title: Add Memento design pattern with UML, code, and examples
description: IMPORTANT GAP: Memento is GoF behavioral, used for undo/redo. Chess problem (in our data) lists it as keyPattern for move history. Undo/redo is core to any editor or interactive tool. Add: UML with Originator, Memento, Caretaker classes. Source: https://singhajit.com/gang-of-four-design-patterns/
acceptanceCriteria: Memento appears in Behavioral category in sidebar
UML diagram with Originator, Memento, Caretaker
TypeScript and Python code with text editor undo example
3 real-world examples (text editor undo, game save states, transaction rollback)
Connection note to Chess LLD problem (move history)

title: Add Visitor design pattern with UML, code, and examples
description: IMPORTANT GAP: Visitor is GoF behavioral. File System problem lists it as keyPattern. Used for operations on composite structures without modifying them. Add: UML with Visitor interface, ConcreteVisitors, Element interface, ConcreteElements. Source: https://singhajit.com/gang-of-four-design-patterns/
acceptanceCriteria: Visitor appears in Behavioral category in sidebar
UML with Visitor (interface), 2 ConcreteVisitors, Element (interface), 2 ConcreteElements
Double dispatch mechanism shown in code
TypeScript and Python code with file system visitor example
3 real-world examples (compiler AST visitors, document exporters, tax calculators)

title: Add Prototype and Bridge design patterns
description: IMPORTANT GAPS: Prototype (creational, used in JS Object.create, game object cloning) and Bridge (structural, decouples abstraction from implementation, used in cross-platform UI). Both GoF patterns missing from module. Add both with full UML, code, examples. Source: https://www.digitalocean.com/community/tutorials/gangs-of-four-gof-design-patterns
acceptanceCriteria: Prototype appears in Creational category with clone() method UML
Bridge appears in Structural category with abstraction/implementation hierarchy
Both have TypeScript and Python code samples
Both have 3 real-world examples and whenToUse/whenNotToUse

title: Add Flyweight and Interpreter design patterns
description: ADVANCED GAPS: Flyweight (structural, memory optimization via shared state) and Interpreter (behavioral, language grammar evaluation). Both GoF but less commonly asked. Add for completeness to achieve 23/23 GoF coverage. Source: https://singhajit.com/gang-of-four-design-patterns/
acceptanceCriteria: Flyweight appears in Structural category with intrinsic/extrinsic state
Interpreter appears in Behavioral category with Expression hierarchy
Both have TypeScript and Python code samples
Both have 3 real-world examples
Module now shows '23 Design Patterns' matching GoF canonical count

title: Add CQRS, Event Sourcing, and Saga as modern patterns
description: EMERGING GAPS: Live 2026 research confirms CQRS (64% read performance gain), Event Sourcing (100% state reconstruction), and Saga (82% distributed consistency) are essential modern patterns. Microservices architecture market projected $15.97B by 2029. Add all 3 as 'Modern' category patterns with UML class diagrams, code samples, and real-world examples. Source: https://microservices.io/patterns/, https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/architecture/patterns/cqrs
acceptanceCriteria: CQRS pattern with Command/Query separation UML, TS/Python code
Event Sourcing with Event Store, Event, Aggregate UML, TS/Python code
Saga with Orchestrator/Choreography variants UML, TS/Python code
All 3 appear in Modern category alongside existing Repository pattern
Each has 3+ real-world examples from microservices context

title: Add UML class diagrams for Circuit Breaker, Bulkhead, Retry, Rate Limiter resilience patterns
description: DEPTH GAP: These 4 patterns exist as behavioral simulations (PatternBehavioralSimulator.tsx) but have NO UML class diagrams with classes/relationships/code. They are simulation-only, not diagram patterns. Add proper UML definitions to patterns.ts so they appear in the Patterns sidebar AND have simulators in the bottom panel. Source: https://microservices.io/patterns/
acceptanceCriteria: Circuit Breaker pattern with CircuitBreaker, State, Config UML classes
Bulkhead pattern with BulkheadConfig, Partition, Semaphore UML classes
Retry pattern with RetryPolicy, BackoffStrategy UML classes
Rate Limiter pattern with TokenBucket, SlidingWindow, Config UML classes
All 4 appear in a new 'Resilience' category in sidebar with UML diagrams
Behavioral Simulator tab still works for all 4

title: Add OOP Fundamentals content — Composition vs Inheritance + Polymorphism
description: CRITICAL GAP: LLD interviews test OOP fundamentals explicitly. 'Composition vs Inheritance' is the #1 OOP question. Polymorphism needs explicit content. Currently these are implicit (shown via UML relationships) but never taught directly. Add: a new 'OOP Fundamentals' section with before/after UML demos similar to SOLID (inheritance → composition refactoring), Polymorphism demo (method overriding in subclasses). Source: https://www.interviewbit.com/low-level-design-interview-questions/
acceptanceCriteria: New sidebar section: 'OOP Fundamentals' or integrated into existing modes
Composition vs Inheritance demo: before (deep hierarchy) → after (composed objects)
Polymorphism demo: base class with 3 subclasses overriding a method
Each has UML diagrams, explanation, real-world analogy (like SOLID demos)
At least Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism, Abstraction, Composition covered

title: Add starter diagrams for 15 SEO-only LLD problems
description: CRITICAL CONTENT GAP: 15 problems in lld-problems-data.ts have SEO pages but NO interactive starter diagrams. Users land on these pages from Google and get text only — no canvas interaction. Problems: Movie Ticket, Restaurant, Airline Booking, Tic-Tac-Toe, Snake Game, Card Game, Notification Service, Logging Framework, Cache System, Task Scheduler, Pub-Sub, Rate Limiter, URL Shortener, Social Media Feed, Spreadsheet, Stock Exchange, Music Streaming. Each needs 3-5 starter UML classes + relationships + hints.
acceptanceCriteria: problems.ts exports all 25 problems with starterClasses/starterRelationships/hints
Each new problem has 3-5 UML classes with realistic attributes and methods
Each has typed relationships matching the keyPatterns in lld-problems-data.ts
Each has 4-5 requirements and 3 progressive hints
All 25 appear in ProblemsBrowser and load interactive diagrams on canvas

title: Add concurrency patterns section — Thread Pool, Producer-Consumer
description: IMPORTANT GAP for LLD interviews: Every FAANG LLD interview involving concurrent systems (parking lot, ticket booking, elevator) tests knowledge of concurrency patterns. Thread Pool and Producer-Consumer are the two most asked. Add as new UML pattern category 'Concurrency' with class diagrams, code samples, and connection to relevant LLD problems. Source: https://www.interviewbit.com/low-level-design-interview-questions/
acceptanceCriteria: New 'Concurrency' category in sidebar with Thread Pool and Producer-Consumer patterns
Thread Pool UML: ThreadPool, Worker, Task (interface), TaskQueue
Producer-Consumer UML: Producer, Consumer, Buffer, Lock
TypeScript and Python code with working implementations
Connection notes to relevant LLD problems (Parking Lot, Movie Ticket, Elevator)

title: Add Activity Diagram support as new diagram type
description: IMPORTANT GAP: Activity diagrams are the 3rd most used UML type in LLD interviews (after class and sequence). Source: https://www.preplaced.in/forums/while-approching-lld-30 — 'start with activity diagram which will help you and the interviewer to be on the same page.' Add: ActivityDiagram type with Action, Decision, Fork/Join, Start/End nodes. 3-5 example activity diagrams for common LLD flows (order processing, user registration, payment flow).
acceptanceCriteria: New 'Activity' tab in sidebar alongside Sequence and State Machine
ActivityDiagram type with Action, Decision, Fork, Join, Start, End node types
SVG canvas renderer for activity diagrams with swim lanes
3+ example activity diagrams (Order Processing, User Registration, Payment)
Properties panel shows node details when clicked

title: Add interview tips section to all 34 design patterns
description: DEPTH GAP: All 34 patterns have whenToUse/whenNotToUse but zero interview-specific guidance. Add: 'interviewTips' field to DesignPattern type with 2-3 tips per pattern (e.g., 'Companies ask Singleton to test thread-safety knowledge', 'Strategy vs State is a classic comparison question', 'Observer is the #1 most asked behavioral pattern'). Source: https://www.ggorantala.dev/crack-big-tech-interviews/
acceptanceCriteria: DesignPattern type extended with interviewTips: string[] field
All 34 patterns have 2-3 interview tips each
Tips displayed in Properties panel under a new 'Interview Tips' section
Tips are actionable ('When asked about Observer, mention the push vs pull variants')

title: Add 'common mistakes' section to all 34 design patterns
description: DEPTH GAP: No pattern warns about common mistakes students make. Add: 'commonMistakes' field to DesignPattern type with 2-3 mistakes per pattern (e.g., 'Singleton: forgetting thread-safety in getInstance()', 'Observer: memory leak from not unsubscribing', 'Factory: returning concrete types instead of interfaces'). Based on recurring errors in code review and student submissions.
acceptanceCriteria: DesignPattern type extended with commonMistakes: string[] field
All 34 patterns have 2-3 common mistakes each
Mistakes displayed in Properties panel with warning icon
Each mistake is specific and actionable (not generic)

title: Add 'vs alternatives' comparison guidance for confusable pattern pairs
description: DEPTH GAP: Strategy vs State, Factory Method vs Abstract Factory, Observer vs Mediator, Adapter vs Bridge, Decorator vs Proxy — these are the most confused pairs. No comparison content exists. Add: comparison data for 6 commonly confused pairs with a structured format via confusedWith field on DesignPattern.
acceptanceCriteria: Comparison data for at least 6 pattern pairs
Accessible from the text-based comparison table (LLD-071)
Each comparison has: Intent, Structure, and 'Use X when... Use Y when...' guidance
Pairs include: Strategy/State, Factory/Abstract Factory, Observer/Mediator, Adapter/Bridge, Decorator/Proxy, Command/Strategy

title: Add 5 more sequence diagram examples for common system interactions
description: DEPTH GAP: Only 5 sequence diagrams exist. LLD interviews frequently ask candidates to draw sequence diagrams for: payment processing with retry, microservice saga coordination, WebSocket real-time updates, GraphQL resolver chain, and distributed transaction with 2PC. Add 5 more examples covering these scenarios.
acceptanceCriteria: 5 new sequence diagrams added to SEQUENCE_EXAMPLES
Payment with Retry: shows retry loop and circuit breaker engagement
Saga Orchestration: shows saga coordinator managing 3 service steps
WebSocket: shows connection upgrade, message push, heartbeat
GraphQL Resolver: shows nested field resolution with batching
Distributed Transaction (2PC): shows prepare and commit phases

title: Add 3 more state machine examples for common system behaviors
description: DEPTH GAP: Only 3 state machines exist (Order, TCP, Traffic Light). Add 3 more covering: (1) User Authentication (logged-out → logging-in → authenticated → locked-out), (2) Payment Processing (pending → processing → succeeded/failed → refunded), (3) CI/CD Pipeline (queued → building → testing → deploying → deployed/failed). These cover interview-relevant domains.
acceptanceCriteria: 3 new state machines added to STATE_MACHINE_EXAMPLES
Authentication: 4+ states with guards for retry limits
Payment: 5+ states with entry/exit actions for notifications
CI/CD Pipeline: 5+ states with parallel paths (build/test)

title: Add AI Agent design patterns section (ReAct, Multi-Agent, Tool Use)
description: EMERGING 2026: Gartner predicts 40% of enterprise apps will use AI agents by 2026. A book 'LLM Design Patterns' was published in 2025. Five core patterns: ReAct, Plan-and-Execute, Multi-Agent Collaboration, Reflection, Tool Use. No LLD tool covers these yet — first-mover advantage. Add as new 'AI Agent' category with UML class diagrams showing agent architectures. Source: https://explore.n1n.ai/blog/5-ai-agent-design-patterns-master-2026-2026-03-21
acceptanceCriteria: New 'AI Agent' category in patterns sidebar
ReAct pattern: Agent, Thought, Action, Observation UML classes
Multi-Agent: Orchestrator, SpecialistAgent, SharedMemory UML classes
Tool Use: Agent, ToolRegistry, Tool (interface), ConcreteTools UML classes
Each has TypeScript code showing agent loop implementation
Real-world examples from LLM application development

title: Add difficulty tags and prerequisite links to all design patterns
description: CURRICULUM GAP: Patterns have no difficulty level and no prerequisites. Students don't know what order to learn them. Add: difficulty (1-5) field to each pattern, suggestedPrerequisites[] field linking to patterns that should be learned first (e.g., Observer before Mediator, Strategy before State, Factory Method before Abstract Factory). Used by learning path features.
acceptanceCriteria: Every pattern has difficulty: 1|2|3|4|5 field
Every pattern has suggestedPrerequisites: string[] with pattern IDs
Difficulty badges visible in PatternBrowser sidebar
Prerequisites shown in Properties panel: 'Learn these first: [links]'
Calibration: Singleton=1, Strategy=2, Observer=2, Mediator=4, Visitor=5
